rulings.Case Title: FORT BRAGG LOCAL BUSINESS MATTERS, AN VS. CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE (PER STIP OF PARTIES AND APPROVAL OF THE COURT)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Monday, February 3, 2025, line 1, PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE Hearing required. The court is inclined to find that the Commission did not abuse its discretion in finding the appeal presented no substantial issue as to the project's conformity with the LCP, at least as to LU-4.1, LU-5.6, LU-5.7, LU-3.5, OS-11.6, OS-16.7, C-1.5, C-6.2, CD-1.1, CD-1.4, CD-1.10, CD-2.5, and CD-5.1. Parties to address OS-12.1 and 12.2, and C-1.4 at oral argument. Respecting OS-12.1 and 12.2, does the policy require preparation of a Water Quality Management Plan in all cases where the development meets criteria specified in the policy, regardless of mitigation? Respecting C-1.4, are there roadway improvements that need to be made for which no timeline, as required by C-1.4, is provided? For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: FREDERICK HENRY HOWARD JR VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Judgment From Arbitration Award
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Monday, February 3, 2025, line 2, PLAINTIFF FREDERICK HOWARD'S Motion To Vacate Judgment From Arbitration Award Plaintiff's motion to vacate judgment is denied. On August 5, 2024, the court concluded that there was no basis to vacate the arbitration award because plaintiff missed the mandatory deadline to seek that relief and confirmed the award in defendant's favor. After the award was confirmed, entry of judgment was a ministerial act. (CCP 1287.4; Caro v. Smith (1997) 59 Cal.App.4th 725, 737.) While entitled a motion to vacate judgment, this motion really seeks to vacate the underlying arbitration award. (See Plaintiff's Motion to Vacate Judgment, 16:14 ["Plaintiff respectfully requests the Final Arbitration Award be vacated."].) Plaintiff has essentially filed an improper motion for reconsideration because he does not cite anything new. (See CCP 1008; Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1094, 1099 ["[a] repeated motion . . . must be based on new facts or law."].) Plaintiff therefore fails to establish any basis to vacate the judgment. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: RAYMOND VINCENT DIGIACOMO JR VS. SHRYNE GROUP, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion And Notice Of Motion To Require Vexatious Litigant Raymond Digiacomo, Jr. To Furnish Security
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Monday, February 3, 2025, line 4, DEFENDANT VIGILANT EYE SECURITY, INC'S Motion To Require Vexatious Litigant Raymond Digiacomo, Jr. To Furnish Security (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Defendant's motion to require vexatious litigant DiGiacomo to furnish a security is granted. Plaintiff DiGiacomo was declared a vexatious litigant on June 9, 2023. (Shryne Group Index of Exhibits, Ex. B.) Pursuant to CCP 391.1, defendant seeks to have plaintiff post a $15,000 security because "there is not a reasonable probability that [plaintiff] will prevail in the litigation against the moving defendant." (CCP 391.1(a).) The court must weigh the evidence to decide whether plaintiff has a reasonable chance of prevailing. (Golin v. Allenby (2010) 190 Cal.App.4th 616, 635.) Plaintiff alleges that Defendant violated the Unruh Act based on his race. (Second Amended Complaint, par. 30.) Plaintiff complains that defendant denied him equal access based on his race because: (1) he was stopped and told he needed to comply with a sign which instructed patrons to put their purchase in plastic bag and (2) threatened that his name would be placed in a punitive database. (Id. at pars. 19-21.) In Mackey v. Board of Trustees of California State University (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 640, 660-661, the court explains that: California's Unruh Act creates a cause of action for any person who is denied the right to "full and equal accommodations, advantages, facilities, privileges, or services in all business establishments of every kind whatsoever" based on that person's "sex, race, color, religion, ancestry, national origin, disability, medical condition, genetic information, marital status, sexual orientation, citizenship, primary language, or immigration status." [citation] The Unruh Act requires proof of intentional acts of race discrimination and does not cover disparate impact. [citations] Here, Defendant's evidence shows that the above-cited conduct was not the result of intentional racial animus. Defendant's personnel never made any racial comments towards plaintiff. (Plaintiff Decl., Ex. 12 [Plaintiff's Depo., at 83:16-84:3; 124:1-127:13.) Defendant's reminder of the bag policy was a reasonable attempt to comply with the law. "All cannabis goods purchased by a customer shall not leave the licensed retailer's premises unless the goods are placed in an opaque exit package." (Cal. Code Regs. tit. 4, 15413(c).) Additionally, Plaintiff's Exhibit 53 demonstrates that notes were made regarding others who refused bags. Defendant shows that Plaintiff does not have a reasonable probability of prevailing. The weight of the evidence shows that Plaintiff's claim is based on a conjectural subjective feeling of discriminatory animus rather than competent proof of invidious racial discrimination. (Plaintiff Decl., Ex. 12 [Plaintiff's Depo., at 83:16-84:3.) The motion is therefore granted. Defendant's request for a $15,000 security is reasonable. Plaintiff shall furnish the security by March 3, 2025. (end of tenative ruling part 1 see part 2) = (301/CV)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Further Verified Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set One; Request For Monetary Sanction Against Plaintiff And His Attorney Julio J. Ramos; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Gayle M. Kono

Ramos The unopposed motion to compel further responses to form interrogatories set one and for sanctions is granted. The court orders Durand to provide further responses to the following form interrogatories: 2.5(a), 2.5(c), 2.6(b), 2.7(a), 2.7(b), 2.11(b), 6.4(c), 6.4(d), 6.5(b), 6.5(c), 6.5(d), 6.5(e), 6.6(c), 6.6(d), 12.4(c), 12.4(d), 12.4(e), 12.4(f), 12.7(a), 12.7(b), 14.1, and 17.1. Responses are due not later than 15 court days from notice of this order. The court does not find Durand acted with substantial justification. Durand shall pay $500 to Plaintiff in sanctions, payment no later than 10 court days from notice of this order. rulings.Case Title: PENSARE, LLC VS. IX LAYER, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Written Discovery Demands And For Discovery Sanctions Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. Section 2023.030; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, February 3, 2025,line 3, DEFENDANT IX LAYER, INC'S Motion To Compel Responses To Written Discovery Demands And For Discovery Sanctions Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. Section 2023.030 (tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) Defendant Ix Layer Inc.'s (IXL) motion to compel responses and sanctions is denied. IXL's opening papers sought written discovery responses from the plaintiff, Pensare LLC (responses to RFPs, form interrogatories, special interrogatories, and requests for admissions) along with sanctions for the fees IXL incurred. IXL's reply papers acknowledge that Pensare has now served responses to the outstanding discovery and that request is moot. IXL also acknowledges that its request for adverse inference sanctions is moot. (Reply at 3 fn. 1.) IXL requests sanctions regardless. The court denies this request because IXL cannot complain about, or seek a sanctions remedy for, extensions that it granted. IXL's motion avers that Pensare negotiated discovery extensions while engaged in bad-faith settlement talks. Whatever the parties' motivations, the moving papers demonstrate that IXL granted an open-ended extension of Pensare's time to respond to written discovery on August 29, 2024. (Zhang Dec. Ex. B.) The notice period to revoke this open-ended extension was not specified by the parties, but on November 27, IXL revoked the open-ended extension and requested written responses, as it was entitled to do (but that revocation left open the deadline for responses and left the deadline ambiguous-a risky course where the consequences for missing a discovery deadline are waiver of objections). On December 23, 2024, IXL specified a new deadline of January 7, 2025, and then agreed on December 27 to another deadline of January 14, 2025. (Zhang Dec. Ex. D.) And the IXL reply brief represents that IXL gave Pensare yet another extension to January 21, 2025. (Reply at 2.) Pensare filed written responses on January 21, 2025. In the meantime, IXL filed the instant motion on December 12, at a time when Pensare's deadline to respond to the discovery was genuinely ambiguous. IXL had revoked the open-ended extension two weeks before and the parties had not discussed a new deadline; presumably they intended a reasonable deadline, but IXL offers no support that for its implicit argument that the deadline was "immediately" or "within two weeks." In any event, that is immaterial now, because IXL later extended the deadline. The court denies IXL's motion; IXL itself mooted its motion by extending the deadline while the motion was pending. By keeping this motion on file and moving out the hearing date while granting extensions to Pensare, IXL appears to have used the present motion as a means to insure Pensare's response. But the Code itself contains a powerful incentive to timely respond-the threat of waiving objections, including objections for attorney-client privilege. And Pensare never said it would not respond; it asked for extensions, which IXL granted. The court encourages IXL not to pursue a similar course in future discovery disputes. Filing a discovery motion as a kind of placeholder while the parties continue to negotiate discovery deadlines wastes judicial resources and also makes the motion a moving target for the court, which is piecing together facts from three different filings coming in at different times. (end of tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: PENSARE, LLC VS. IX LAYER, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT IX LAYER, INC'S MOTION TO COMPEL RESPONSES TO WRITTEN DISCOVERY DEMANDS AND FOR DISCOVERY SANCTIONS PURSUANT TO CAL. CODE CIV. PROC. SECTION 2023.030 (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, February 3, 2025, line 3, DEFENDANT IX LAYER, INC'S MOTION TO COMPEL RESPONSES TO WRITTEN DISCOVERY DEMANDS AND FOR DISCOVERY SANCTIONS PURSUANT TO CAL. CODE CIV. PROC. SECTION 2023.030 (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2) Pensare has not sought sanctions, but the discovery act states that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." (CCP 2031.300(c).) Analogous provisions apply to the other discovery tools at issue here, interrogatories and RFAs. The court does not find that IXL acted with substantial justification here and awards $500 sanctions, payable to Pensare, against IXL. Sanctions shall be paid within 10 days of notice of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (end of tentative ruling part 2 of 2) = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: KEONI GANDALL VS. TRILOBIO INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Form Interrogatories And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, February 3, 2025, line 4, 3 - DEFENDANTS TRILOBIO INC., ROYA AMINI-NAIENI, MAXIMILIAN SCHOMMER'S Motion To Compel Responses To Form Interrogatories And For Sanctions The court grants the motion to compel further form interrogatory responses, denies the motion to deem objections waived, and grants sanctions. The requests at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP 2017.010.) Trilobio has shown Gandall's responses are incomplete. Gandall has not demonstrated that any objection has merit. The court orders Gandall to provide further responses to Trilobio's form interrogatories, set one, within 10 court days from notice of entry of this order. The court denies the motion to deem Gandall's objections waived; a timely response preserves objections even where the receiving party deems them non-substantive. The court does not find Gandall acted with substantial justification. Gandall shall pay $5,040 to Plaintiff in sanctions, payment no later than 10 court days from notice of entry of this order. rulings.Case Title: KEONI GANDALL VS. TRILOBIO INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Deem The Truth Of All Matters In Trilobio Requests For Admission Be Admitted And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, February 3, 2025,, line 4, 1 - DEFENDANTS TRILOBIO INC., ROYA AMINI-NAIENI, MAXIMILIAN SCHOMMER'S Motion To Deem The Truth Of All Matters In Trilobio Requests For Admission Be Admitted And For Sanctions The court grants the motion to compel further RFA responses, denies the motion to deem RFAs admitted, and grants sanctions. The requests at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP Section 2017.010.) Trilobio has shown Gandall's responses are incomplete. Gandall has not demonstrated that any objection has merit. The court orders Gandall to provide further responses to RFAs set 1, Nos. 1-10 within 10 court days from notice of entry of this order. The court denies the motion to deem the RFAs admitted. Trilobio characterizes Gandall's responses untimely because the responses provided by the extended deadline were not substantive, a purported condition of the extension agreement. The Discovery Act provides a "deemed admitted" remedy only where responses are not timely served and does not condition that remedy on substance. (CCP 2023.280(a).) The court does not find Gandall acted with substantial justification. Gandall shall pay $5,040 to Plaintiff in sanctions, payment no later than 10 court days from notice of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: MARIAH MCBRIDE VS. IGS SOLUTIONS LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendant Igs Solutions Llc Medical Group, Inc. To Further Respond To Set One Of Plaintiff'S Form Interrogatories - General, And Special Interrogatories; Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $3,575.00; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof; Declaration Of Elen Martirosyan
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, February 3, 2025, line 5, PLAINTIFF MARIAH MCBRIDE'S Motion To Compel Defendant Igs Solutions Llc Medical Group, Inc. To Further Respond To Set One Of Plaintiff'S Form Interrogatories - General, And Special Interrogatories; Request For Sanctions In The Amount Of $3,575.00; (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Plaintiff Mariah McBride's motion to compel further responses to special interrogatories set 1 and employment form interrogatories set 1, and for sanctions, is granted in part and denied in part. With respect to form interrogatory 209.2, the motion is granted. McBride has equal access to court documents and records, but not equal access to information about which cases contain those records. Defendant IGS Solutions LLC's objection is overruled and IGS is ordered to amend its response to this interrogatory within 10 court days of the date of notice of entry of this order. The court finds McBride's meet and confer efforts were sufficient as to this interrogatory. With respect to special interrogatories 36 through 126, the court denies the motion to compel further responses for McBride's failure to meet and confer adequately under all the facts presented here. CCP 2030.040 permits a party to propound additional special interrogatories beyond 35 with contemporaneous service of a supporting declaration containing substantially the language of CCP 2030.050. Once a propounding party serves that supporting declaration, the responding party is obliged to respond, "[s]ubject to the right of the responding party to seek a protective order under Section 2030.090." (CCP 2030.040(a).) In other words, the responding party may seek a protective order to avoid responding, or it may respond, including by asserting objections. The supporting declaration here was substantially in the form prescribed by CCP 2030.050. While it was boilerplate, it complied with the language of the statute. IGS responded by submitted only objections, contending that the declaration was insufficient to require it to provide a substantive response. In meet and confer efforts, just before the motion to compel deadline expired, IGS clarified that it was seeking "justification based on the needs of this case." (Ex. 9 to Gehsgian Dec.) IGS would not extend the deadline on McBride's motion to compel, and McBride did not provide justification. (end of tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: MARIAH MCBRIDE VS. IGS SOLUTIONS LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF MARIAH MCBRIDE'S MOTION TO COMPEL DEFENDANT IGS SOLUTIONS LLC MEDICAL GROUP, INC. TO FURTHER RESPOND TO SET ONE OF PLAINTIFF'S FORM INTERROGATORIES - GENERAL, AND SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES; REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS IN THE AMOUNT OF $3,575.00; (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, February 3, 2025, line 5, PLAINTIFF MARIAH MCBRIDE'S MOTION TO COMPEL DEFENDANT IGS SOLUTIONS LLC MEDICAL GROUP, INC. TO FURTHER RESPOND TO SET ONE OF PLAINTIFF'S FORM INTERROGATORIES - GENERAL, AND SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES; REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS IN THE AMOUNT OF $3,575.00; (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2) CCP 2030.050 states in part that a declaration in support of additional interrogatories should include the following language: "8. This number of questions is warranted under Section 2030.040 of the Code of Civil Procedure because __________. (Here state each factor described in Section 2030.040 that is relied on, as well as the reasons why any factor relied on is applicable to the instant lawsuit.)" IGS's objections essentially pointed out that McBride had not done so sufficiently in this case. McBride's meet and confer response was essentially to insist that the language of the declaration was enough. Restating a position is not a "reasonable and good faith attempt" to resolve discovery issues informally. (CCP 2016.040, 2030.300(b).) Such a good faith attempt could have entailed explaining in further detail why the additional interrogatories were warranted; that did not occur here. In view of the volume of discovery served in this case, and in light of all the facts set out in the parties' declarations, something more was required here, and the court finds McBride's meet and confer attempts to be inadequate. Both parties seek sanctions. The court finds that McBride did not act with substantial justification and awards $1000 sanctions payable by McBride to IGS within 10 court days of the date of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (end of tentative ruling part 2 of 2) = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: DANIELLE CHEEK VS. NTT DATA SERVICES, LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff-Appellant Danielle Cheek To Answer Request For Production Of Documents, Set One, And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Monday, February 3, 2025, line 8, RESPONDENT NTT DATA SERVICES, LLC A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff-Appellant Danielle Cheek To Answer Request For Production Of Documents, Set One, And Request For Sanctions (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) The motion to compel request for production responses and for sanctions is granted. Defendant NTT Data Services LLC served written requests for production on plaintiff Danielle Cheek. A party to whom such a discovery request is made is obliged to respond unless the party obtains a protective order. CCP 2031.210(a). Cheek has not responded. The fact that she is out of state does not excuse her lack of response. The fact that NTT previously had an opportunity to view documents that are now being requested does not relieve Cheek of the obligation to respond. Having failed to respond, Cheek has waived objections to the requests for production. (CCP 2031.300(a).) The court understands that Cheek is representing herself, but Cheek is obliged nonetheless to comply with civil procedure rules. Moreover, Cheek appears to be asserting that she will return for trial in this matter but because she is out of state until then she has no obligation to comply with pretrial procedural requirements. That proposition is not supported by the law and would not accord procedural fairness to both sides. Cheek is ordered to respond to the written requests for production without objection and to produce responsive documents within 15 court days of notice of entry of this order. NTT also requests sanctions. CCP 2031.300(c) provides that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." The court finds that Cheek did not act with substantial justification here but also finds it would be unjust to award the full amount requested by NTT. Cheek is ordered to pay $100 in sanctions to NTT within 15 court days of notice of entry of this order. (end of tentative rulign part 1, see part 2) = (301/CV)
